title: Building a Team Chat / Messaging App in Laravel
subtitle: Channels and DMs, real-time delivery over WebSockets, message ordering and cursor pagination, unread counts, read receipts, presence, and fan-out — the Laravel architecture behind a Slack-style chat.

sections.h2: How a chat app fits together

blocks.p: A chat app is a real-time fan-out problem over an append-only message log. The pressure: one message must reach every channel member instantly, history must paginate cleanly while new messages keep arriving, and each user needs an accurate unread count. Persistence is easy; delivery and counting are hard.

blocks.diagram:
Workspace
 ├─ Users
 ├─ Channels (public / private)  ── members (pivot, last_read_at)
 │   └─ Messages (append-only, ordered)
 │       ├─ Reactions
 │       ├─ Thread replies
 │       └─ Attachments
 └─ Direct Messages (a channel of 2+, no name)

blocks.p: The defining action — sending a message — persists, then fans out over WebSockets:

blocks.diagram:
User sends ──► POST /channels/{id}/messages
   │ authorize (member?)
   ▼
 Persist Message (gets id + created_at)   ◄ source of truth
   │
   ▼ broadcast MessageSent to presence channel chat.{id}
   │     (all connected members receive instantly via Reverb)
   ▼
 Queue: push notifications to offline members, bump unread

ul: Data model — workspaces, channels (incl. DMs as nameless channels), append-only messages, membership pivot.
Real-time delivery — broadcasting MessageSent to a per-channel channel via Reverb/Pusher.
Ordering & pagination — cursor (keyset) pagination so infinite scroll is stable as new messages arrive.
Unread & presence — last_read_at per member for counts; presence channels for who's online.

blocks.callout: A chat app is fan-out delivery over an append-only log. The hard parts are real-time delivery to all members, stable cursor pagination, and accurate per-user unread counts — not storing the message.
blocks.title: The mental model in one sentence

blocks.p: A channel groups messages and has members through a pivot. A direct message is just a channel with no name and a fixed membership — modelling DMs as channels avoids a parallel message table. The pivot row per member carries last_read_at, the key to unread counts and read receipts.

Messages are an immutable log: you insert, rarely update (edits keep an edited_at), and soft-delete. Order by (id) or (created_at, id). Treating the table as a log — not mutable rows — is what makes pagination and real-time consistent.

When a message is saved, you broadcast(new MessageSent($message)) to a channel named chat.{channelId}. Laravel Reverb (a first-party WebSocket server) or Pusher/Ably delivers it to every connected member's browser. The HTTP response and the broadcast are separate paths — the sender sees their own message from the response (or echo), others from the broadcast.

Offset pagination (LIMIT 50 OFFSET 100) breaks in chat: new messages arriving shift every offset, causing duplicates or skips, and large offsets are slow. Cursor / keyset pagination (where id < :lastSeenId order by id desc limit 50) is stable regardless of inserts and uses the index directly. Essential for infinite scroll.

Per member, unread = messages in the channel newer than their last_read_at. Don't recount the whole table on every request — maintain a counter or compute with an indexed count where created_at > last_read_at. Mark-as-read updates last_read_at when the user views the channel.

Read receipts derive from each member's last_read_at vs a message's timestamp. Presence (who's online/typing) uses a presence channel — a broadcast channel that tracks subscribers; typing indicators are ephemeral client events, never persisted.

Connected users get the WebSocket broadcast. Offline members need push/email — dispatched from a queued job after the message saves, respecting mute settings. Separate the two paths: real-time for present users, queued notifications for absent ones.

blocks.callout: Offset pagination is the classic chat bug: as messages arrive, offsets shift and users see duplicates or gaps. Use cursor/keyset pagination keyed on message id.
blocks.title: Most common misconception

blocks.p: Chat persists an append-only message log, broadcasts each new message to all channel members over WebSockets, paginates history by cursor, and tracks unread via each member's last_read_at.

blocks.h4: Analogy — the bulletin board with a town crier

blocks.p: The message log is a bulletin board where notices are only ever pinned (append-only), never reordered. The broadcast is a town crier who, the instant a notice goes up, shouts it to everyone currently in the square (connected members) — but people who went home (offline) get a letter later (queued notification). Unread is each person remembering the time they last read the board; anything newer is unread. Cursor pagination is reading the board from a remembered notice downward, so newly pinned notices at the top never make you lose your place.

blocks.diagram:
Message saved
   ├─ ONLINE members  ─► Reverb broadcast (instant, in-memory)
   └─ OFFLINE members ─► queued job ─► push / email (mute-aware)

blocks.p: Each lesson is one idea. Code illustrative.

blocks.h3: Lesson 3 — Broadcast on a presence channel

items.q: Q1. Why model direct messages as channels rather than a separate table?
items.a: A DM is structurally a channel with no name and fixed membership. Reusing channels avoids a parallel message table, duplicated pagination/broadcast logic, and special-casing — DMs and group chats share one code path.

items.q: Q2. Why is offset pagination wrong for chat history?
items.a: New messages arriving shift every offset, so paging produces duplicates or skipped messages, and deep offsets scan many rows. Cursor/keyset pagination (id < lastSeen) is stable under inserts and uses the index.

items.q: Q3. What are the two separate delivery paths for a new message?
items.a: (1) Real-time broadcast over WebSockets (Reverb/Pusher) to currently connected members. (2) A queued job sending push/email to offline members (respecting mutes). Present vs absent users are served differently.

items.q: Q4. How is a per-user unread count computed, and what makes it cheap?
items.a: Count messages in the channel newer than the member's last_read_at (from the pivot). An index on (channel_id, created_at) keeps it fast; mark-as-read updates last_read_at when the user views the channel.

items.q: Q5. Why are messages treated as append-only?
items.a: An immutable log makes ordering, cursor pagination, and real-time consistency simple. Edits keep an edited_at and deletes are soft — the sequence of ids/timestamps never changes underneath readers.

items.q: Q6. What kind of broadcast channel powers presence and why?
items.a: A presence channel — it tracks who is subscribed, so you get the online roster for free and can layer typing indicators. Channel auth returns a user payload for members, false for non-members.

items.q: Q7. Why should typing indicators not be persisted?
items.a: They're ephemeral, high-frequency, and worthless after the moment. Send them as client whispers/whisper events over the socket; persisting would hammer the DB for no value.

blocks.p: Re-do the quiz from memory; redraw the two delivery paths each session.

head: When
Session (~10–15 min)
Goal
rows: Read packet; quiz with answers open.
Flashcards; explain cursor vs offset aloud.
Interleaved quiz; write the unread-count query from blank.
Teach the fan-out problem to someone in 5 minutes.
Closed-book interleaved pass; misses restart Day 1.

blocks.callout: Retrieving just before you'd forget produces the strongest gain.
blocks.title: Why the gaps grow

blocks.p: Topics shuffled to train choosing the right idea.

items.q: M1. Users scrolling up sometimes see the same message twice. Cause and fix?
items.a: Offset pagination shifting as new messages arrive. Switch to cursor/keyset pagination keyed on message id (where id < lastSeenId order by id desc).

items.q: M2. Messages save fine but other members don't see them live. Where do you look first?
items.a: Broadcasting, not the DB. Check the WebSocket server (Reverb running), the channel authorization callback for chat.{id}, that MessageSent implements ShouldBroadcast, and the client Echo subscription.

items.q: M3. Unread badges are slow on busy workspaces. Two fixes?
items.a: (1) Index (channel_id, created_at) and count against last_read_at rather than scanning. (2) Maintain a denormalized unread counter per membership updated on send/read, or compute counts in a batched query.

items.q: M4. Offline users miss messages entirely — no push, no email. What's missing?
items.a: The second delivery path. Dispatch a queued job after save to notify absent members (push/email), mute-aware. Real-time broadcast only reaches connected clients.

items.q: M5. A non-member connected to a channel's socket and received messages. Security flaw?
items.a: Presence/private channel authorization not enforcing membership. The Broadcast::channel callback must return false for non-members; never trust the client's channel name alone.

items.q: M6. Typing indicators are flooding the database with writes. Redesign?
items.a: Don't persist them. Send as client whispers over the existing socket connection (ephemeral); they never touch the DB.

ul: Build pagination twice — offset vs cursor — and reproduce the duplicate-message bug, then watch cursor fix it.
Break delivery on purpose — disable the queued notify job and confirm offline users get nothing; then restore it.
Write the membership/auth tests first: non-members can't subscribe, can't post, can't read.
Add threads — replies referencing a parent message id, with a per-thread unread.
Stretch goals: message search (Scout/Meilisearch), reactions, edit/delete with live sync, presence-based online roster, and horizontal scaling of Reverb behind a load balancer.

flashcards: The three hard parts of chat
Real-time fan-out delivery, stable cursor pagination, accurate per-user unread counts.
What a chat app fundamentally is
Fan-out delivery over an append-only message log.
Why DMs are modelled as channels
A DM is a nameless channel with fixed membership; reuse avoids a parallel message table and duplicate logic.
Why offset pagination fails in chat
Arriving messages shift offsets → duplicates/skips; deep offsets are slow.
Cursor pagination query
where id < :lastSeenId order by id desc limit N — stable and index-backed.
Two delivery paths for a message
Reverb broadcast to online members; queued push/email to offline members (mute-aware).
How unread is computed
Count messages newer than the member's last_read_at (pivot), with index on (channel_id, created_at).
Mark-as-read mechanism
Update last_read_at on the channel_user pivot when the user views the channel.
Why messages are append-only
Immutable log keeps ordering, pagination, and real-time consistent; edits/deletes are flags.
Channel type for presence/typing
Presence channel — tracks subscribers (online roster) and carries whisper events.
Where typing indicators live
Ephemeral client whispers over the socket — never persisted.
Broadcasting ->toOthers()
Skips echoing the message back to the sender, who already has it from the response.
glossary: A container of messages with members; a DM is a nameless channel.
The append-only, ordered table of messages.
Pushing server events to clients over WebSockets.
A broadcast channel that tracks who is subscribed.
Keyset paging using a stable key (message id) instead of offset.
Per-member timestamp marking how far they've read.
Messages newer than a member's last_read_at.
Derived from comparing last_read_at to a message timestamp.
An ephemeral client-to-client socket event (e.g. typing).
Delivering one message to many recipients.
A member setting suppressing notifications for a channel.
